ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a concise and practically effective combination — the SIF a/(a+p(w)) weighting for word vectors plus removal of the first principal component — and supplies a probabilistic derivation from a modified generative discourse model. These elements are not mere re-statements of standard tf-idf or unweighted averaging and the theoretical grounding distinguishes it from prior empirical heuristics; the method’s ability to match supervised models on many benchmarks is also a surprising empirical claim. While individual pieces (weighted averages, component removal) have antecedents, the specific weight formula, its derivation, and the demonstrated robustness across corpora/embeddings represent a notable and non-trivial contribution rather than a purely incremental combination. | B(4): The paper proposes a simple but conceptually new combination—SIF weighting of word vectors plus removal of the top principal component—and crucially derives the weighting from a modified generative model for discourse, which offers a novel theoretical perspective linking a simple heuristic to a probabilistic model. The problem (sentence embeddings) is well-studied, but the explicit analytic justification and the surprisingly strong empirical success across many benchmarks differentiate it from prior averaging and supervised embedding methods. The techniques are not a complex algorithmic leap, yet they constitute a non-trivial and insightful extension beyond standard unweighted or tf-idf averaging and prior supervised fine-tuning, so the contribution would meaningfully inform experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concise and practically effective combination — the SIF a/(a+p(w)) weighting for word vectors plus removal of the first principal component — and supplies a probabilistic derivation from a modified generative discourse model. These elements are not mere re-statements of standard tf-idf or unweighted averaging and the theoretical grounding distinguishes it from prior empirical heuristics; the method’s ability to match supervised models on many benchmarks is also a surprising empirical claim. While individual pieces (weighted averages, component removal) have antecedents, the specific weight formula, its derivation, and the demonstrated robustness across corpora/embeddings represent a notable and non-trivial contribution rather than a purely incremental combination.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a simple but conceptually new combination—SIF weighting of word vectors plus removal of the top principal component—and crucially derives the weighting from a modified generative model for discourse, which offers a novel theoretical perspective linking a simple heuristic to a probabilistic model. The problem (sentence embeddings) is well-studied, but the explicit analytic justification and the surprisingly strong empirical success across many benchmarks differentiate it from prior averaging and supervised embedding methods. The techniques are not a complex algorithmic leap, yet they constitute a non-trivial and insightful extension beyond standard unweighted or tf-idf averaging and prior supervised fine-tuning, so the contribution would meaningfully inform experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear, testable claims and supports them with a simple algorithm, theoretical derivation from a generative discourse model, and empirical results across many STS and downstream tasks. The math derivation appears plausible and aligns with the weighting rule, but the summary omits details about proof completeness and whether assumptions (e.g., unigram estimates, common discourse model) were stress-tested. Experimental methodology is strong in breadth and baselines, yet the summary lacks explicit mention of full ablations, seed control, released code/preprocessing, and statistical significance testing—leaving some reproducibility and attribution questions. Overall the conclusions are largely justified, with only minor concerns about isolating which components drive gains and ensuring exact reproducibility. | B(4): The paper's main empirical claims are well supported: across many STS benchmarks and downstream tasks the SIF-weighted average plus first-PC removal consistently improves over unweighted averaging and matches or outperforms several strong baselines, and hyperparameters are reported. The theoretical derivation linking the SIF weight to a modified random-walk-on-discourses model is plausible and illuminates why downweighting common words and removing a common discourse direction helps, but it relies on stylized assumptions (e.g., a single dominant common component and unigram-frequency-based smoothing) whose fidelity to real text is not fully validated. Experimental methodology is broadly sound with appropriate baselines and diverse datasets, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or ablations that would strengthen some claims. Overall the work is technically solid with minor gaps in validating theoretical assumptions and reporting of statistical robustness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and supports them with a simple algorithm, theoretical derivation from a generative discourse model, and empirical results across many STS and downstream tasks. The math derivation appears plausible and aligns with the weighting rule, but the summary omits details about proof completeness and whether assumptions (e.g., unigram estimates, common discourse model) were stress-tested. Experimental methodology is strong in breadth and baselines, yet the summary lacks explicit mention of full ablations, seed control, released code/preprocessing, and statistical significance testing—leaving some reproducibility and attribution questions. Overall the conclusions are largely justified, with only minor concerns about isolating which components drive gains and ensuring exact reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are well supported: across many STS benchmarks and downstream tasks the SIF-weighted average plus first-PC removal consistently improves over unweighted averaging and matches or outperforms several strong baselines, and hyperparameters are reported. The theoretical derivation linking the SIF weight to a modified random-walk-on-discourses model is plausible and illuminates why downweighting common words and removing a common discourse direction helps, but it relies on stylized assumptions (e.g., a single dominant common component and unigram-frequency-based smoothing) whose fidelity to real text is not fully validated. Experimental methodology is broadly sound with appropriate baselines and diverse datasets, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or ablations that would strengthen some claims. Overall the work is technically solid with minor gaps in validating theoretical assumptions and reporting of statistical robustness.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—the SIF weighting formula and first-PC removal—are stated early and described clearly. Notation appears consistent and important implementation details (datasets, hyperparameters, unigram estimation sources) are reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are generally effective and the writing is good, but the presentation could be improved by explicitly stating code availability and expanding a few limitation discussions (e.g., antonymy/sentiment failure modes) to better guide practitioners. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: the SIF-weighted average plus common-component removal and its theoretical motivation from a generative discourse model. Notation for the SIF weights and PC subtraction is concise and appears consistent, and the experimental tables/figures across many datasets (STS, SICK, SNLI, SST, IMDB, etc.) are informative for comparing to strong baselines. Writing is generally clear and readable, with thorough related work coverage, though a few technical derivations could be expanded for readers less familiar with the generative framing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—the SIF weighting formula and first-PC removal—are stated early and described clearly. Notation appears consistent and important implementation details (datasets, hyperparameters, unigram estimation sources) are reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are generally effective and the writing is good, but the presentation could be improved by explicitly stating code availability and expanding a few limitation discussions (e.g., antonymy/sentiment failure modes) to better guide practitioners.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: the SIF-weighted average plus common-component removal and its theoretical motivation from a generative discourse model. Notation for the SIF weights and PC subtraction is concise and appears consistent, and the experimental tables/figures across many datasets (STS, SICK, SNLI, SST, IMDB, etc.) are informative for comparing to strong baselines. Writing is generally clear and readable, with thorough related work coverage, though a few technical derivations could be expanded for readers less familiar with the generative framing.

main_claims: A very simple sentence embedding method—weighted average of word vectors with smooth inverse frequency (SIF) weights followed by removal of the first principal component—substantially improves over unweighted averaging and often outperforms or matches more complex supervised models on textual similarity tasks.

The paper provides a theoretical justification for the SIF weighting by deriving it from a modified random-walk-on-discourses generative model for text that includes smoothing terms and a common discourse component.

The proposed method is unsupervised, robust across choices of word vectors and frequency corpora, and produces embeddings that are effective features for downstream supervised tasks (comparable or better than several baselines).
_summary.methodology: Compute sentence vector as the weighted average of word embeddings with weight a/(a+p(w)) (SIF), where p(w) are estimated unigram probabilities, then compute the first principal component across sentence vectors and subtract its projection from each sentence vector (common component removal).
datasets: enwiki (used for word vectors and p(w) estimation)
metrics: Pearson's correlation (primary evaluation for textual similarity)
limitations: The method ignores word order, which can matter (authors show RNN/LSTM performance drops when words are shuffled).

Antonymy and sentiment issues: distributional embeddings and the weighting can downweight important words like 'not', hurting sentiment tasks; thus it underperforms specialized models on some sentiment benchmarks.
On rare cases (e.g., MSRpar) the method can decrease performance.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - sentence/phrase embeddings and representation learning

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP representation models in both academic and industry settings; experience includes reproducible benchmark design, ablation studies, and production deployment of sentence embedding systems.
traits: focuses on experimental rigor: requests clear baselines, hyperparameters, dataset splits, and statistical significance
values reproducibility: looks for code, pre-trained vectors, exact preprocessing, and seed control
expects comprehensive ablations to isolate effects (e.g., weight formula vs. unigram estimates vs. PC removal)
notices implementation details that affect results (tokenization, OOV handling, corpus mismatch) and evaluates robustness claims empirically
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critically examines the empirical methodology and reproducibility, seeking clear demonstrations that each claimed component contributes to results and that comparisons are fair.

reviewer_b.name: Representation Learning & Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor studying sentence and discourse representations with a track record of work on unsupervised methods and theoretical analyses; advises research agendas and evaluates broader significance for the NLP community.
traits: evaluates novelty and theoretical contribution relative to prior literature, including classical and recent embedding methods
considers practical impact: simplicity, computational cost, and how the method shifts research or practice
attends to clarity of assumptions in theoretical derivations and how they map to real text data
values insights that unify disparate approaches (e.g., connecting generative models to simple heuristics) and assesses generality across tasks
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the work's novelty, theoretical soundness, and potential community impact, balancing appreciation for elegant ideas with scrutiny of their actual significance and scope.
